fuzzy_prd:
Our team needs a self-contained utility library that helps application developers safely assemble and inspect the two main text-based query languages used by centralized identity services. The first concern is neutralizing special characters inside arbitrary user-supplied strings before those strings are embedded in queries or address paths — the escaping mode should be selectable so only the characters relevant to the target context are touched, and specific characters can optionally be passed through unchanged. The second concern is building search queries from a declarative list of conditions rather than hand-writing fragile text: conditions can require an exact match, a range, set membership, or substring presence, they can be combined with AND or OR logic, nested into explicit logical groups, or supplemented with verbatim pre-built fragments. Every built query must expose both a wire-safe form and a human-readable form. The third concern is working with hierarchical address strings that identify individual entries in the directory tree: the library must decompose such an address into its constituent parts and expose named scalar views of the first component and the parent path, validate whether a string is a well-formed address, compare two addresses to determine ancestor or child relationships, and support an ordered sequence of non-destructive edits such as reversing, trimming from either end, or attaching new components whose reserved characters are automatically neutralized. Finally, a parser must convert an existing query string back into a structured node tree — following the internal node representation used by the group and condition types — and reassemble it into canonical form; malformed input must surface as a dedicated error signal rather than a silent failure. The team has flagged the escaping-mode selection logic and the DN hierarchy comparison as the two areas most likely to cause subtle bugs.

One extra detail on escaping since that came up: if the 'mode' field is missing, it should default to 'all', and in that mode we escape every byte of the value, not just reserved characters. Each byte is rendered as a backslash plus its two lowercase hexadecimal digits, so 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' becomes '\61\62\63\64\65\66\67\68\69\6a\6b\6c\6d\6e\6f\70\71\72\73\74\75\76\77\78\79\7a'. Also, the optional 'ignore' field is just a list of characters that should pass through untouched even if they would normally be escaped. With value 'ab' and ignore 'a', the output is 'a\62'. The ignore string is passed directly to the underlying ldap_escape function as its second argument. For the implementation touchpoints people asked me to call out, this is the EscapedValue class in src/Models/Attributes/EscapedValue.php and the mode dispatch in rcb_tests/dispatcher/Dispatcher.php (the 'escape' case calling ->dn(), ->filter(), ->both(), or leaving flags=0 for 'all').

On the query builder side, a term with a 'raw' key should be inserted verbatim into the filter with no escaping at all. Both query= and unescaped= need to show that raw string unchanged. The concrete example here is raw '(field=value)' producing 'query=(field=value)
unescaped=(field=value)
'. Raw fragments still join into AND/OR groupings the same way normal conditions do, so they’re not a separate path, just an unescaped one.

For dn_parts, the output format needs to stay very literal. It returns exactly nine lines in this order: 'components=<json_array>', 'rdns=<json_array>', 'values=<json_array>', 'multi=<json_array_of_pairs>', 'assoc=<json_object>', 'name=<string>', 'head=<string>', 'relative=<string>', 'parent=<string>'. The assoc object maps each lowercased attribute to an array of its values, and the 'multi' array contains [attribute, value] pairs. All JSON is encoded with JSON_UNESCAPED_SLASHES | JSON_UNESCAPED_UNICODE flags. If parent is null or empty, return an empty string, not 'null'. And if the input is malformed or not a distinguished name at all, we still return the full shape, just empty: 'components=[]
rdns=[]
values=[]
multi=[]
assoc={}
name=
head=
relative=
parent=
'.

For dn_validate, it reports exactly two booleans, 'valid=' and 'empty='. Important nuance there: anything invalid is also treated as empty, so if valid=false then empty=true. A bare word like 'invalid' gives 'valid=false
empty=true
'. A well-formed pair like 'cn=john doe' gives 'valid=true
empty=false
'. We should tolerate whitespace around the attribute, the equals sign, and the whole string when deciding validity.

For dn_relate, the output is exactly four lines in this fixed order: 'ancestor_of=<bool>', 'descendant_of=<bool>', 'parent_of=<bool>', 'child_of=<bool>'. Booleans are lowercase 'true' and 'false'. Identical names are neither ancestor nor descendant. Empty or non-DN inputs should return all false. Comparisons are case-insensitive for both attribute names and values. The relationship semantics are also pretty specific: parent_of=true means the second name is exactly one level below the first, meaning the first name’s components are a suffix of the second with exactly one extra component at the front of the second. ancestor_of=true is the broader any-depth version. So for 'dc=bar' vs 'cn=foo,dc=bar': ancestor_of=true, parent_of=true. For 'dc=bar' vs 'cn=foo,ou=bar,dc=bar': ancestor_of=true, parent_of=false. The code areas tied to this are the isChildOf(), isParentOf(), isAncestorOf(), isDescendantOf() methods in src/Models/Attributes/DistinguishedName.php, which compare lowercased component arrays after stripping the leading component for child/parent checks.

One last DN builder clarification: the 'reverse' step flips the order of all components, but it’s applied at serialization time rather than mutating the stored component order in place. So with 'cn=john,dc=local,dc=com' plus a reverse step, the output is 'dn=dc=com,dc=local,cn=john
', and any later steps still operate on the original order. Also, when appending or prepending a component, reserved characters in the value should be auto-escaped using the DN escaping mode. Example: appending attribute 'dc' with value ',' should produce 'dc=\2c' in the final DN, and for 'cn=john,dc=local,dc=com' with append dc=',' the full output is 'dn=cn=john,dc=local,dc=com,dc=\2c
'.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each escaped byte is rendered as a backslash followed by exactly two lowercase hexadecimal digits. For example, a comma ',' becomes '\2c', a left parenthesis '(' becomes '\28', and an asterisk '*' becomes '\2a'. Uppercase hex letters are never used. The output string ends with a trailing newline character.

trigger_keywords: default filter when terms list is empty
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'terms' list is empty (or absent) in a filter request, the system produces a default presence filter on the object-class attribute. The output is 'query=(objectclass=*)\nunescaped=(objectclass=*)\n'. The attribute name 'objectclass' is used literally with no escaping in this default case.

A 'present' match produces '(attr=*)' — the value is a literal asterisk with no escaping applied. An 'absent' match produces '(!(attr=*))' — a negation group wrapping the presence test. Both query= and unescaped= lines show the same text for presence/absence because the asterisk is not subject to value escaping in these cases.

trigger_keywords: bool or with single and condition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When exactly one condition has the default conjunction (no 'bool' field or 'bool':'and') and exactly one condition has 'bool':'or', the entire query becomes a single OR group containing both conditions: '(|(cond1)(cond2))'. The conjunctive condition is listed first. There is no outer AND wrapper in this specific 1-and + 1-or case.

A lone condition tagged with 'bool':'or' (with no other conditions present) collapses to a single bare condition with no surrounding group operator — it behaves identically to a lone conjunctive condition. For example, a single or-tagged equals condition on 'cn' produces '(cn=<value>)' not '(|(cn=<value>))'.

A term with an 'in' key containing a 'field' and a 'values' array produces an OR group of equality conditions, one per value, in list order. For example, field 'name' with values ['john','mary','sue'] produces 'query=(|(name=\6a\6f\68\6e)(name=\6d\61\72\79)(name=\73\75\65))\nunescaped=(|(name=john)(name=mary)(name=sue))\n'. Each value is byte-escaped in the query line.

A term with a 'between' key containing 'field', 'from', and 'to' produces a conjunction: '(&(field>=<escaped_from>)(field<=<escaped_to>))'. The greater-or-equal condition comes first, the less-or-equal condition second. Both bounds are byte-escaped in the query line. For example, 'from':'20161001000000.0Z' escapes to '\32\30\31\36\31\30\30\31\30\30\30\30\30\30\2e\30\5a'.

A term with a 'group' key accepts three values: 'all' yields an AND group '(&...)', 'any' yields an OR group '(|...)', and 'none' yields a NOT group '(!...)'. The inner 'terms' array of the group is processed recursively using the same rules. Any other value for 'group' throws an InvalidArgumentException.

A structurally invalid filter (unbalanced parentheses, unclosed nested group, or malformed condition) causes the parse_filter operation to output exactly one line: 'error=parser_exception\n'. This is the only output — no partial nodes= or assembled= lines are emitted. The error is triggered by catching a ParserException; all other exceptions are not caught by this mechanism.

The nodes= line contains a JSON array. Each group node is serialized as {"group": "<operator>", "nodes": [...]} where operator is '&', '|', or '!'. Each leaf condition node is serialized as {"attribute": "<attr>", "operator": "<op>", "value": "<val>"}. The assembled= line contains the canonical filter string rebuilt from the node tree, with redundant surrounding parentheses and insignificant whitespace removed but spaces inside values preserved.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: named scalar views of the first component and the parent path
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The name(), head(), relative(), and parent() methods on DistinguishedName in src/Models/Attributes/DistinguishedName.php; parent() returns null (rendered as empty string) when there are no remaining components after shifting

context_pointers.vague_reference: the internal node representation used by the group and condition types
context_pointers.actual_meaning: GroupNode and ConditionNode classes in src/Query/Filter/GroupNode.php and src/Query/Filter/ConditionNode.php, used by Parser::parse() and serialized by rcb_node_to_array() in the dispatcher

context_pointers.vague_reference: a dedicated error signal rather than a silent failure
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ParserException class in src/Query/Filter/ParserException.php, caught in the dispatcher's parse_filter case to emit 'error=parser_exception\n'

oracle_data.fallback_response: This toolkit covers five operations selected by the 'op' field: 'escape' (neutralize special characters in a value), 'filter' (build a search query from declarative terms), 'dn_parts' (decompose a hierarchical address string), 'dn_validate' (check structural validity of a hierarchical address), 'dn_relate' (compare two addresses for ancestor/descendant/parent/child relationships), 'dn_build' (transform an address through ordered steps), and 'parse_filter' (parse a query string into a node tree and reassemble canonically). Please rephrase your question to target one of these specific behaviors, the output format of a particular operation, or the handling of edge cases such as empty inputs, reserved characters, or invalid structures.

feature1_escape_value.description: Escape an arbitrary string so it can be safely embedded inside an LDAP distinguished name or search filter. A mode selects which reserved set to neutralize: "all" escapes every byte, "dn" escapes only characters reserved within a distinguished name, "filter" escapes only characters reserved within a search filter, and "both" escapes the union of the two sets. An optional set of characters may be marked as ignored so they pass through unescaped. Each escaped byte is rendered as a backslash followed by its two lowercase hexadecimal digits; unescaped bytes are emitted verbatim. The output is the resulting escaped string.

feature2_1_single_conditions.description: Compile a single attribute condition into an LDAP search filter. A condition names an attribute, a match kind, and (for value-bearing matches) a value. The output reports two lines: "query=" carries the wire filter with the value escaped byte-by-byte for transmission, and "unescaped=" carries the same filter with values shown in their readable form. Equality wraps as (attr=value); negated equality wraps as (!(attr=value)); approximate, greater-or-equal and less-or-equal use ~=, >= and <=; presence tests use (attr=*) and its negation (!(attr=*)); substring matches place a wildcard before, after, or on both sides of the value, each with a negated variant. When no condition is supplied a default presence filter on the object-class attribute is produced. Attribute names containing reserved characters are themselves escaped.

feature2_2_combining_conditions.description: Combine multiple conditions into one filter. Conditions default to conjunction; a condition may instead be tagged as disjunctive. Two or more conjunctive conditions are wrapped in an AND group (&...). Two or more disjunctive conditions are wrapped in an OR group (|...). A lone disjunctive condition collapses to a single bare condition with no surrounding group. When exactly one conjunctive condition is mixed with exactly one disjunctive condition, the whole query becomes a single OR group of the two. When several conjunctive conditions coexist with several disjunctive ones, the conjunctive conditions sit at the top level of an AND group and the disjunctive ones are nested together inside an OR group within it.

feature2_3_set_and_range.description: Build convenience filters over a single attribute. A set-membership request lists candidate values for one attribute and produces a disjunction matching any of them: an OR group of equality conditions, one per value. A range request gives a lower and upper bound for one attribute and produces a conjunction of a greater-or-equal condition and a less-or-equal condition. Output reports the escaped wire filter and its unescaped readable form.

feature2_4_nested_and_raw.description: Compose nested filter groups and inject pre-built raw filter fragments. A group request wraps a set of inner conditions inside a single logical operator: "all" yields an AND group, "any" yields an OR group, and "none" yields a NOT group. Groups may be combined with each other and with top-level conditions, producing properly nested wire output. A raw fragment is inserted verbatim with no escaping, and raw fragments combine with ordinary conditions following the same conjunction/disjunction wrapping rules. Output reports the escaped wire filter and its unescaped readable form.

feature3_1_dn_decompose.description: Decompose a distinguished name into its parts. The output reports, as JSON arrays: the components (each "attribute=value" pair, with values still escaped), the relative distinguished names, the bare values in order, a list of [attribute, value] pairs, and an associative grouping mapping each lowercased attribute to the list of its values (case-insensitive merge). It also reports four scalar views: the value of the first (leftmost) pair, the attribute name of the first pair, the leftmost component as a string, and the parent (everything except the leftmost component). A malformed or non-distinguished-name input yields empty collections and empty scalar views.

cases.expected_output:
components=["cn=John Doe","ou=foo","dc=bar","dc=baz"]
rdns=["cn=John Doe","ou=foo","dc=bar","dc=baz"]
values=["John Doe","foo","bar","baz"]
multi=[["cn","John Doe"],["ou","foo"],["dc","bar"],["dc","baz"]]
assoc={"cn":["John Doe"],"ou":["foo"],"dc":["bar","baz"]}
name=John Doe
head=cn
relative=cn=John Doe
parent=ou=foo,dc=bar,dc=baz

feature3_2_dn_validate.description: Determine whether a string is a structurally valid distinguished name, and whether the normalized form is empty. A value is valid when it contains at least one well-formed attribute=value pair; bare words, an empty attribute, an empty value, or surrounding prose make it invalid. Whitespace around the attribute, the equals sign, and the whole string is tolerated. The output reports "valid=" and "empty=" as booleans, where an invalid value is always treated as empty.

feature3_3_dn_hierarchy.description: Compare two distinguished names for hierarchical relationships, matching attribute names and values case-insensitively. The output reports four booleans for the first name relative to the second: ancestor_of (the second is nested somewhere beneath the first), descendant_of (the first is nested somewhere beneath the second), parent_of (the second is exactly one level beneath the first), and child_of (the first is exactly one level beneath the second). Identical names are neither ancestor nor descendant of one another; empty or non-distinguished-name inputs yield all false.

feature4_dn_builder.description: Edit a distinguished name through an ordered sequence of immutable-style steps and read back the result. Steps: "reverse" flips the order of all components; "shift" removes a given count of components from the front; "pop" removes a given count from the end (removing more than present clears it); "append" adds a component at the end; "prepend" adds a component at the front. An added component may be given as an attribute plus a value, as a single "attribute=value" string, or as a list mixing both forms; surrounding whitespace on attribute and value is trimmed, and reserved characters in the value are escaped. The output reports the final distinguished name string.

feature5_parse_filter.description: Parse an LDAP search filter string into a node tree and reassemble it into canonical form. The output reports "nodes=", a JSON tree where each group node is {group: operator, nodes: [...]} and each leaf condition is {attribute, operator, value}, followed by "assembled=", the canonical filter rebuilt from that tree. Reassembly drops redundant surrounding parentheses and insignificant whitespace while preserving spaces inside values, and supports several sibling root nodes. A structurally invalid filter (unbalanced parentheses, an unclosed nested group, or a malformed condition) is reported as a single neutral error line "error=parser_exception".
